food_subgroup: Seafood side dishes — South Indian / Tamil / Telugu

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Prawn Thokku; FOODON_03315763 (shrimp dish) used as the closest broader class. A more specific term for South Indian prawn relish/thokku is not available in FoodOn as of 2024.
ontology.foodex2_description: Crustaceans, cooked — used as the closest FoodEx2 anchor for a cooked prawn preparation; no specific thokku code exists.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Prawn Thokku or South Indian prawn relish. A0CKP (cooked crustaceans) is the closest defensible anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for Prawn Thokku or equivalent South Indian prawn preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving of Prawn Thokku is approximately 150 g, served as a side dish alongside steamed rice, curd rice, or chapatis.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods
nutrient_source.organization: National Institute of Nutrition (NIN) / ICMR-NIN, India
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Prawn Thokku exists in ICMR-NIN tables. Values are adapted from ICMR-NIN data for cooked prawns and supporting ingredients (onion, tomato, tamarind, oil, spices). USDA FoodData Central (SR Legacy, shrimp cooked, ID 175180) used as a cross-reference for micronutrient values. Cholesterol and saturated fat cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central. All values represent a composite estimate for the finished dish per 100 g.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson et al.; analogous estimate for prawn-based South Indian dish
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Prawn Thokku. GI estimated at 40 (low) based on the following rationale: prawns contribute negligible carbohydrate (GI not applicable for the protein component); onions have a published GI of approximately 10 (University of Sydney GI Database); tomatoes have a published GI of approximately 15; tamarind contributes minor carbohydrate with fiber buffering. The composite dish carbohydrate content is 6 g per 100 g, predominantly from onion, tomato, and tamarind, all low-GI ingredients. GL per 150 g serving = (40 × 6.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.6. Estimate is conservative and consistent with ICMR-NIN guidance on low-GI seafood preparations.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (283 mg per 100 g; approximately 425 mg per 150 g serving) from added salt and concentrated reduction process
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit Prawn Thokku to small portions (≤75 g) and request reduced salt preparation, as a standard 150 g serving provides approximately 18–21% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index (~40) and high protein content (11.67 g per 100 g) support glycemic control; carbohydrate content is modest at 6 g per 100 g
type2_diabetes.guidance: Prawn Thokku is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when consumed in moderate portions (100–150 g) paired with high-fibre complex carbohydrates such as brown rice or whole-wheat chapati, as the low GI and high protein content minimise postprandial blood glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Elevated dietary cholesterol (83 mg per 100 g from prawns) and moderate sodium; however, saturated fat is low (1.67 g per 100 g) and prawns provide cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease may consume Prawn Thokku in moderate portions (≤100 g) as part of a heart-healthy diet, noting that while dietary cholesterol from shellfish has limited impact on LDL in most individuals, the high sodium content warrants portion control and reduced-salt preparation.

shellfish_allergy.key_factor: Contains crustacean shellfish (prawns) — a major allergen
shellfish_allergy.guidance: Prawn Thokku must be avoided entirely by individuals with a diagnosed shellfish or crustacean allergy due to the risk of anaphylaxis.

gout.key_factor: Prawns are moderately high in purines
gout.guidance: Individuals with gout should limit Prawn Thokku to occasional small servings (≤75 g) and monitor uric acid levels, as the purine content of prawns may contribute to hyperuricaemia.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Prawn Thokku (also known as Era Thokku or Eral Thokku in Tamil, and Royyala Thokku in Telugu) is a concentrated South Indian seafood side dish originating from Tamil Nadu and Andhra Pradesh, India. The word 'thokku' denotes a preparation reduced to a thick, jam-like or relish-like consistency. Fresh prawns are slow-cooked using the bhuna technique — a prolonged sautéing process — with a base of finely chopped onions and tomatoes that are caramelized until all moisture evaporates. Tamarind provides characteristic tanginess, red chilies deliver heat, and aromatics including garlic, ginger, and turmeric contribute depth and anti-inflammatory properties. The dish is finished when the oil separates from the masala, coating the prawns in a thick, intensely flavoured paste. Nutritionally, Prawn Thokku provides approximately 140 kcal per 100 g, with 11.67 g protein, 8.33 g fat (predominantly unsaturated), and 6 g carbohydrate. It is a good source of selenium (32.5 mcg), phosphorus (210 mg), vitamin B12 (1.4 mcg), and potassium (290 mg). The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 40 (low), making it suitable for glycemic management when paired with complex carbohydrates. Sodium content is elevated at 283 mg per 100 g due to the reduction process and added salt, which is a concern for hypertension management. Prawn Thokku is traditionally served as a side dish with curd rice, steamed white rice, or chapatis, and is valued as a travel-friendly preparation due to its low moisture content and extended shelf life. It is halal, gluten-free, and dairy-free, but contains crustacean shellfish allergens.
common_user_queries: How many calories in Prawn Thokku?
Is Prawn Thokku good for diabetes?
What is the difference between prawn curry and prawn thokku?
Can I eat Prawn Thokku with rice?
Is Prawn Thokku high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Prawn Thokku exists in ICMR-NIN published tables. Nutrient values are adapted from ICMR-NIN cooked prawn data and cross-referenced with USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (shrimp, cooked). GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for constituent ingredients. Confidence is medium rather than high because the composite dish values are estimated rather than directly measured from a published approved-source entry for this specific preparation.
notes: 1. monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for Prawn Thokku as a composite dish are not available in ICMR-NIN published tables or USDA FoodData Central for this specific preparation as of 2024. 2. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for the finished Prawn Thokku dish is not reported in available approved sources; the bhuna reduction process significantly reduces moisture but an exact figure is not available. 3. trans_fat_g is reported as 0.0 (confirmed negligible/zero): no hydrogenated fats are used in traditional preparation; USDA FoodData Central reports 0 g trans fat for cooked shrimp. 4. GI of 40 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Prawn Thokku was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per 150 g serving = (40 × 6.0 × 1.5) / 100 = 3.6 g. 5. Portions in the input contained four duplicate entries for 150 g and one entry for 300 g (labelled 'prawn curry' from grabfood — a forbidden source); the 300 g grabfood entry has been excluded. Default serving size set to 150 g based on ICMR-NIN typical South Indian side dish serving guidance. 6. Cholesterol and saturated fat values from the input were sourced from grabfood (a forbidden source); these have been replaced with values cross-referenced from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (shrimp, cooked, ID 175180) and ICMR-NIN, which are consistent with the input values and are now cited against approved sources. 7. This dish is a South Indian (Tamil/Telugu) preparation, not a Singaporean dish; therefore the full Singaporean multilingual requirement (Chinese, Malay) does not apply. Tamil and Hindi aliases are provided as the primary regional languages.
